A {@code Joiner} may cancel the scope (sometimes + * called "short-circuiting") when some condition is reached, e.g. a subtask fails, that + * does not require the outcome of other subtasks that are still executing. Cancelling the + * scope prevents new threads from being started in the scope, cancels subtasks in the + * scope that have not completed execution, and causes the {@code join()} method to wake up + * with the outcome (result or exception). In the above example, the outcome is that {@code + * join()} completes normally when all subtasks succeed. The scope is cancelled if any + * subtask fails and {@code join()} throws {@code ExecutionException} with the exception + * from the failed subtask as the {@linkplain Throwable#getCause() cause}. It is invoked with the subtask + * in the {@code UNAVAILABLE} state. If the method throws an exception or error then + * it is propagated by the {@code fork(Callable)} method without creating a thread to + * execute the subtask.
